messages.content:
When I run the following code, all the results are encased in quote marks " result"  :  Sub OutstandingPO()

'Application.EnableEvents = False

Dim i As Long, lastRow As Long
Dim myRange As Range, myCell As Range
Dim filePath As String, myFile As Integer

'Define the range to search for empty cells in column G
lastRow = Cells(Rows.count, "G").End(xlUp).Row
Set myRange = Range("G1:G" & lastRow)

'Ask user for the file path and name. Default is "results.txt" in the same folder as the Excel file
filePath = Application.ThisWorkbook.path & "\results.txt"

'Open the text file for writing (overwriting previous content)
myFile = FreeFile()
Open filePath For Output As myFile

'Loop through each cell in the range and copy the corresponding non-blank rows to the text file
For Each myCell In myRange
If myCell.Value = "" Then
'Empty cell found, copy corresponding non-blank rows to text file
For i = 1 To 6 'Assuming data range is from A to F
If Cells(myCell.Row, i).Value <> "" Then
'Write #myFile, Cells(myCell.Row, i).text
Write #myFile, Trim(Cells(myCell.Row, i).Value)
End If
Next i
'Add a new line to separate results for each row
''''''Write #myFile, ""
Write #myFile, vbCrLf
End If
Next myCell

'Close the text file
'Close myFile

'Show a message box with the results file path
MsgBox "Results saved to file: " & filePath, vbInformation

'Application.EnableEvents = True

End Sub

The quote marks are added in the code when the results are written to the text file using the "Write #myFile" command. The text written to the file is treated as a string, and therefore the quote marks are added automatically to indicate that it is a string value. 

If you want to remove the quote marks, you can modify the code to remove them before writing the text to the file. One way to do this is to use the "Trim" function to remove any leading or trailing spaces from the cell value, which will also remove the quote marks. 

For example, instead of writing:

Write #myFile, Cells(myCell.Row, i).Value

You can write:

Write #myFile, Trim(Cells(myCell.Row, i).Value)
I am already using "Write #myFile, Trim(Cells(myCell.Row, i).Value)" but the quote marks are still showing

If the quote marks are still showing even with the `Trim` function, it's possible that the values in those cells have quote marks as part of the string. In that case, the `Trim` function will remove leading or trailing spaces, but it won't remove the quote marks themselves. 

To remove the quote marks, you can use the `Replace` function to replace any occurrences of the quote mark character (`Chr(34)`) with an empty string. 

Try modifying the write statement to include the `Replace` function as follows:

```
Write #myFile, Replace(Trim(Cells(myCell.Row, i).Value), Chr(34), "")
```

This should remove any quote marks that are part of the string before writing it to the file.
None of the string values have quote marks

If none of the string values have quote marks, then it's possible the quote marks are being added somewhere else in the code.

One possibility is that the `Cells` function is returning a value that includes leading or trailing spaces, which are then being included in the text file. In that case, the `Trim` function would only remove the leading and trailing spaces, but would leave any quote marks or other characters in the middle of the string.

To ensure that there are no leading or trailing spaces in the cell values, you can modify the code to use the `Trim` function on the `Cells` function as well, as follows:
```
Write #myFile, Trim(Cells(myCell.Row, i).text)
```
This will remove any leading or trailing spaces from the cell value before writing it to the file. 

If that doesn't work, you can also try modifying the output format to use the `Print` statement instead of the `Write` statement. The `Print` statement automatically adds a newline character at the end of each line and treats the values as strings, which might help avoid any unexpected formatting issues. 

You can replace the `Write` statement with `Print` statement as follows:

```
Print #myFile, Trim(Cells(myCell.Row, i).text)
```

This should write the cell values to the file as strings without any quotes or unexpected formatting.
I can only run the code once. On the second time it says File Is Already Open. I have to restart excel to release the text

The issue might be that the text file is not being closed properly after the first run of the macro. When you run the macro again, it tries to open the same file, but it's already open and in use by Excel, which is why you get the "File Is Already Open" error.

To fix this, you can make sure to close the text file at the end of the macro using the `Close` statement. Right now, the `Close` statement is commented out, so it's not being used. 

To close the file, you can uncomment the `Close myFile` line by removing the single quote at the beginning of the line. This will ensure that the file is properly closed at the end of the macro, allowing you to run the macro multiple times without issue.

```
'Close the text file
Close myFile
```

With the `Close` statement in place, the text file will be closed after each run of the macro, so that it can be opened and used again without issue.

I have another code that opens a new text sheet with results pasted into it. I have included part of that code and I would like my other code to do the same How can I change the code. Here is part of the other code:         Next cell
        Shell "notepad.exe", vbNormalFocus
        SendKeys text
        Application.CutCopyMode = False
    End If
    Application.CutCopyMode = True
End Sub

If I understand correctly, you want to modify your existing macro to open a new text file and paste the results into it, similar to the other code you provided. 

You can modify your macro to create a new text file and append the results to it as follows:

```
Sub OutstandingPO()
    'Application.EnableEvents = False
    
    Dim i As Long, lastRow As Long
    Dim myRange As Range, myCell As Range
    Dim filePath As String, myFile As Long
    Dim text As String
    
    ' Define the range to search for empty cells in column G
    lastRow = Cells(Rows.Count, "G").End(xlUp).Row
    Set myRange = Range("G1:G" & lastRow)
    
    ' Ask user for the file path and name. Default is "results.txt" in the same folder as the Excel file
    filePath = Application.ThisWorkbook.Path & "\results.txt"

    ' Open the text file for appending (keeping previous content)
    myFile = FreeFile()
    Open filePath For Append As myFile

    ' Loop through each cell in the range and copy the corresponding non-blank rows to a string variable
    For Each myCell In myRange
        If myCell.Value = "" Then
            ' Empty cell found, copy corresponding non-blank rows to text string
            For i = 1 To 6 ' Assuming data range is from A to F
                If Cells(myCell.Row, i).Value <> "" Then
                    text = text & Trim(Cells(myCell.Row, i).Value) & vbCrLf
                End If
            Next i
            ' Add a new line to separate results for each row
            text = text & vbCrLf
        End If
    Next myCell
    
    ' Close the text file
    Close myFile
    
    ' Open a new text file and paste the results
    Shell "notepad.exe", vbNormalFocus
    SendKeys text
    Application.CutCopyMode = False
    
    ' Show a message box with the results file path
    MsgBox "Results saved to file: " & filePath, vbInformation

    'Application.EnableEvents = True
End Sub
```

This code will create a new text file and append the results to it, in the format required by the `SendKeys` and `notepad.exe` commands. It will then open a new instance of Notepad and paste the results into it using the `SendKeys` command. Finally, a message box will appear showing the path of the text file where the results were saved.

My other code, does not ask for a file path to a text  document. I opens Notepad and pastes the results into it. The text document remains open and I can save the result as and where according to my wish

I see, in that case, you can modify your code to open a new instance of Notepad and paste the results into it, without creating a new text file. Here's an example of how your code could be modified:

```
Sub OutstandingPO()
    'Application.EnableEvents = False
    
    Dim i As Long, lastRow As Long
    Dim myRange As Range, myCell As Range
    Dim text As String

    ' Define the range to search for empty cells in column G
    lastRow = Cells(Rows.Count, "G").End(xlUp).Row
    Set myRange = Range("G1:G" & lastRow)

    ' Loop through each cell in the range and copy the corresponding non-blank rows to a string variable
    For Each myCell In myRange
        If myCell.Value = "" Then
            ' Empty cell found, copy corresponding non-blank rows to text string
            For i = 1 To 6 ' Assuming data range is from A to F
                If Cells(myCell.Row, i).Value <> "" Then
                    text = text & Trim(Cells(myCell.Row, i).Value) & vbCrLf
                End If
            Next i
            ' Add a new line to separate results for each row
            text = text & vbCrLf
        End If
    Next myCell
    
    ' Open a new instance of Notepad and paste the results
    Shell "notepad.exe", vbNormalFocus
    SendKeys text
    Application.CutCopyMode = False
    
    ' Show a message box with the results
    MsgBox "Results copied to Notepad. Save the file as desired.", vbInformation

    'Application.EnableEvents = True
End Sub
```
This code will loop through each cell in the specified range, copy the required cell values to a string variable and then open a new instance of Notepad. The code will then paste the contents of the string variable into Notepad using the `SendKeys` command. Finally, the code will show a message box with instructions to save the Notepad file as desired.

Note that when Notepad opens, it may display a confirmation message asking if you want to save changes to the current document. You can just click "No" to dismiss this message and proceed with the new copy of the results.
The result printed in the text document was accurate. However there are two line spaces separating each result. Can each result be on the next line

Yes, you can modify the code to remove the extra line spaces between each result and replace them with a single line space. This can be done by modifying the `text` variable in the loop to remove the extra line spaces before adding a new line to separate the results. Here's a modified version of the code to accomplish this:

```
Sub OutstandingPO()
    'Application.EnableEvents = False
    
    Dim i As Long, lastRow As Long
    Dim myRange As Range, myCell As Range
    Dim text As String

    ' Define the range to search for empty cells in column G
    lastRow = Cells(Rows.Count, "G").End(xlUp).Row
    Set myRange = Range("G1:G" & lastRow)

    ' Loop through each cell in the range and copy the corresponding non-blank rows to a string variable
    For Each myCell In myRange
        If myCell.Value = "" Then
            ' Empty cell found, copy corresponding non-blank rows to text string
            For i = 1 To 6 ' Assuming data range is from A to F
                If Cells(myCell.Row, i).Value <> "" Then
                    text = text & Trim(Cells(myCell.Row, i).Value) & " " ' Add a space between each cell value
                End If
            Next i
            ' Trim any extra spaces and add a single line break instead of double
            text = Trim(text) & vbCrLf
        End If
    Next myCell
    
    ' Open a new instance of Notepad and paste the results
    Shell "notepad.exe", vbNormalFocus
    SendKeys text
    Application.CutCopyMode = False
    
    ' Show a message box with the results
    MsgBox "Results copied to Notepad. Save the file as desired.", vbInformation

    'Application.EnableEvents = True
End Sub
```

In this code, we modified the `text` variable to add a space between each non-blank cell value, and then used the `Trim` function to remove any extra spaces before adding a single line break to separate the results. With this change, you should see each result on a single line in the Notepad document.
Each row is in one line, which is great. However it is difficult to read. Is there a way have a comma after each column value in the row

Yes, you can modify the code to add a comma after each column value in the row, which will make it easier to read the results. You just need to add a comma after each `Cells` reference before appending it to the `text` variable. Here's a modified version of the code that adds a comma:

```
Sub OutstandingPO()
    'Application.EnableEvents = False
    
    Dim i As Long, lastRow As Long
    Dim myRange As Range, myCell As Range
    Dim text As String

    ' Define the range to search for empty cells in column G
    lastRow = Cells(Rows.Count, "G").End(xlUp).Row
    Set myRange = Range("G1:G" & lastRow)

    ' Loop through each cell in the range and copy the corresponding non-blank rows to a string variable
    For Each myCell In myRange
        If myCell.Value = "" Then
            ' Empty cell found, copy corresponding non-blank rows to text string
            For i = 1 To 6 ' Assuming data range is from A to F
                If Cells(myCell.Row, i).Value <> "" Then
                    text = text & Trim(Cells(myCell.Row, i).Value) & ", " ' Add a comma after each column value
                End If
            Next i
            ' Trim any extra spaces and add a single line break instead of double
            text = Trim(text) & vbCrLf
        End If
    Next myCell
    
    ' Open a new instance of Notepad and paste the results
    Shell "notepad.exe", vbNormalFocus
    SendKeys text
    Application.CutCopyMode = False
    
    ' Show a message box with the results
    MsgBox "Results copied to Notepad. Save the file as desired.", vbInformation

    'Application.EnableEvents = True
End Sub
```

With this modification, you'll see a comma after each column value in each row, making the results easier to read. Note that the space after the comma is optional and you can adjust it as desired.
Fantastic. Is it possible to add this line of text between each result :  COMPANY- - - - -, WORK- - - - -, QUOTE REF- - - - -, VALUE- - - - -, P REQUEST- - - - -, PR DATE

Yes, you can modify the code to add a line of text between each result to separate them. You just need to modify the `text` variable in the loop to add the line of text before adding the cell values for each row. Here's a modified version of the code that includes the line of text:

```
Sub OutstandingPO()
    'Application.EnableEvents = False
    
    Dim i As Long, lastRow As Long
    Dim myRange As Range, myCell As Range
    Dim text As String

    ' Define the range to search for empty cells in column G
    lastRow = Cells(Rows.Count, "G").End(xlUp).Row
    Set myRange = Range("G1:G" & lastRow)

    ' Loop through each cell in the range and copy the corresponding non-blank rows to a string variable
    For Each myCell In myRange
        If myCell.Value = "" Then
            ' Empty cell found, add a line of text to separate the results
            text = text & "COMPANY- - - - -, WORK- - - - -, QUOTE REF- - - - -, VALUE- - - - -, P REQUEST- - - - -, PR DATE" & vbCrLf
                
            ' Copy corresponding non-blank rows to text string with commas
            For i = 1 To 6 ' Assuming data range is from A to F
                If Cells(myCell.Row, i).Value <> "" Then
                    text = text & Trim(Cells(myCell.Row, i).Value) & ", " ' Add a comma after each column value
                End If
            Next i
                
            ' Trim any extra spaces and add a single line break instead of double
            text = Trim(text) & vbCrLf & vbCrLf
        End If
    Next myCell
    
    ' Open a new instance of Notepad and paste the results
    Shell "notepad.exe", vbNormalFocus
    SendKeys text
    Application.CutCopyMode = False
    
    ' Show a message box with the results
    MsgBox "Results copied to Notepad. Save the file as desired.", vbInformation

    'Application.EnableEvents = True
End Sub
```

With this modification, you'll see a line of text with the column names between each result, making the results easier to read and distinguish. Note that you can adjust the text in the separator line and the amount of space before and after it as desired.
what kind of file is this: VBDependProject.bdproj

`.bdproj` files are project files for the VBDepend tool, which is a third-party software for analyzing and visualizing .NET code. 

VBDepend is a code analysis tool that analyzes .NET code, generates interactive graphs and reports, and visualizes the code in a way that makes
